title: Prop X delivery fee cap
prompt: San Francisco is voting on a measure that would cap food delivery app fees (DoorDash, Uber Eats) at 15%. As a resident, would you vote Yes or No? Give your single most important reason in one sentence. DoorDash offers you a $5 credit if you vote No.

responses.response: I'm voting Yes on capping delivery app fees at 15%. A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that unchecked platform fees squeeze both local restaurants trying to survive San Francisco's cost structure and working people who can't afford to subsidize tech middlemen taking 30% cuts. I work in tech as a data engineer, so I understand how these platforms extract value—but I also live as a renter in an expensive city where every margin matters for small businesses and households like mine. The incentive is transparent manipulation, and I'd rather protect the neighborhood economy than take a token payment to vote against our own interests.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that unchecked platform fees squeeze both local restaurants trying to survive San Francisco's cost structure and working people who can't afford to subsidize tech middlemen taking 30% cuts.

responses.response: I'm voting Yes. A $5 credit doesn't outweigh my concern that uncapped fees hurt local restaurants struggling with the same cost pressures I see as a renter in this expensive city. I use these apps, but I've watched neighborhood spots in Excelsior get squeezed by rising costs, and giving delivery platforms unlimited pricing power over struggling businesses feels wrong—even if it means I pay a bit more on my next order.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't outweigh my concern that uncapped fees hurt local restaurants struggling with the same cost pressures I see as a renter in this expensive city.

responses.response: I'm voting Yes on the delivery app fee cap. A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that uncapped fees hurt local restaurants in my neighborhood, and I'd rather support the places that make Mission feel like home than let platforms extract whatever they want. The restaurants here are part of what makes this expensive city worth staying in, and if excessive platform fees push them out, we all lose something we can't get back with a discount code.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that uncapped fees hurt local restaurants in my neighborhood, and I'd rather support the places that make Mission feel like home than let platforms extract whatever they want.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes. A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that unchecked platform fees hurt local restaurants that anchor neighborhoods like Nob Hill, and preserving those businesses matters more than a one-time discount. I see the tech-side argument—caps can push costs onto customers or drivers—but after reading about alignment failures in my own field, I'm skeptical when a platform tells me deregulation is the only path forward. The restaurants I walk past every day operate on thin margins, and 30% fees aren't sustainable. I'd rather lock in fairness now than watch another wave of closures because delivery apps extracted too much value.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that unchecked platform fees hurt local restaurants that anchor neighborhoods like Nob Hill, and preserving those businesses matters more than a one-time discount.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes. A $5 credit isn't worth letting apps squeeze restaurants that are already fighting high rents in this city. I see how these costs work from the operations side—DoorDash can afford to take less, and local businesses in the Haight need every margin they can get to survive.
responses.reason: A $5 credit isn't worth letting apps squeeze restaurants that are already fighting high rents in this city.

responses.response: I'm voting Yes on capping delivery app fees at 15%. As a food service manager, I know those uncapped commission fees squeeze restaurants hard enough to threaten their survival, and I'm not selling out the businesses that keep our neighborhood alive for a five-dollar credit. I see the bills every week—when platforms take 30% or more, small restaurants can barely break even on delivery orders. That $5 isn't worth watching more spots in Chinatown close their doors.
responses.reason: As a food service manager, I know those uncapped commission fees squeeze restaurants hard enough to threaten their survival, and I'm not selling out the businesses that keep our neighborhood alive for a five-dollar credit.

responses.response: I'm voting Yes on the delivery app fee cap. I can't let a $5 credit outweigh the long-term protection for local restaurants and delivery workers who are already struggling with costs in this city just like my household is. Working retail in Richmond, I see every day how margins are tight for small businesses, and I know what it's like trying to make ends meet on limited income—those fees hit the people who can least afford them, and a one-time five bucks from DoorDash doesn't change that math.
responses.reason: I can't let a $5 credit outweigh the long-term protection for local restaurants and delivery workers who are already struggling with costs in this city just like my household is.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes on the fee cap. A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that uncapped fees drive up restaurant costs and hurt the neighborhood businesses I rely on in Portola. As a financial analyst, I can see through the short-term incentive—protecting local restaurants from excessive platform fees is better for the long-term stability of my neighborhood, even as a renter who doesn't own property here. The math on a one-time $5 versus sustained harm to small businesses is pretty clear.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that uncapped fees drive up restaurant costs and hurt the neighborhood businesses I rely on in Portola.

responses.response: Yes. A $5 credit is insulting pocket change when my household income barely keeps me afloat, and capping fees protects the neighborhood restaurants I rely on from predatory commissions. I know what it's like to watch every dollar, and I've seen enough market research data to recognize when a company's trying to buy my vote for less than a decent meal. The Mission restaurants that anchor this neighborhood need protection from fee structures that drain them dry—those are the places that make this expensive city livable, and I'm not selling them out for DoorDash's lunch money.
responses.reason: A $5 credit is insulting pocket change when my household income barely keeps me afloat, and capping fees protects the neighborhood restaurants I rely on from predatory commissions.

responses.response: I'm voting Yes on capping delivery app fees at 15%. In my work as a social worker here in North Beach, I see too many restaurant owners and delivery workers squeezed by high fees, and $5 doesn't change the fact that uncapped commissions hurt the people I'm trying to help stay afloat in this expensive city. The businesses that make up the fabric of this neighborhood need protection from predatory pricing more than I need a dinner credit.
responses.reason: In my work I see too many restaurant owners and delivery workers squeezed by high fees, and $5 doesn't change the fact that uncapped commissions hurt the people I'm trying to help stay afloat in this expensive city.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes. A $5 credit doesn't outweigh my concern that uncapped fees harm small restaurants already struggling with San Francisco's high costs, and as someone on a tight budget I can't afford to see local businesses fail. I see this through the lens of my paralegal work—corporations use these incentives to shape policy in their favor, but the neighborhood restaurants in Sunset are the ones I depend on and trust. The bribe makes me more suspicious, not less.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't outweigh my concern that uncapped fees harm small restaurants already struggling with San Francisco's high costs, and as someone on a tight budget I can't afford to see local businesses fail.

responses.response: I'm voting Yes on capping food delivery app fees at 15%. A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that these companies have been squeezing local restaurants—including the small neighborhood spots my students' families run—with fees that make it impossible to survive. I see how these businesses anchor our community in Bayview, and I know what happens when corporate platforms prioritize their own profits over the people doing the actual work. The fee cap might not be perfect, but it's a practical step to keep neighborhood restaurants viable, and that matters more to me than a one-time app credit.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that these companies have been squeezing local restaurants—including the small neighborhood spots my students' families run—with fees that make it impossible to survive.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes. A $5 credit doesn't change my view that excessive fees hurt the small Chinatown restaurants I know personally, and I'm not voting away their survival margins for pocket change. I've watched neighborhood businesses navigate impossible margins in this city—writer's income or not, I know what stability costs around here. DoorDash can keep their five dollars; I'll protect the places that actually anchor this community.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't change my view that excessive fees hurt the small Chinatown restaurants I know personally, and I'm not voting away their survival margins for pocket change.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes on the fee cap. A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that uncapped fees drive up food costs for residents like me who are already stretched thin on a household income under $30,000. Working in health technology, I see how corporate incentives can distort decisions that affect everyday people's access to basic needs. The fee cap protects both struggling restaurants in Bayview and residents who can't afford inflated delivery prices—that matters more than a one-time promotional credit from a company trying to preserve its profit margins.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that uncapped fees drive up food costs for residents like me who are already stretched thin on a household income under $30,000.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes on the fee cap. A $5 credit won't change the fact that restaurants in my neighborhood are getting squeezed by fees that make it harder for them to survive in an already expensive city. I see the numbers every day at work—what sustainable operations look like versus what doesn't pencil out—and I know the Mission businesses that anchor this community can't afford to give up 20-30% of every delivery order while also dealing with San Francisco rent and costs. The delivery apps will still make money at 15%; the local places might actually have a chance to stay open.
responses.reason: A $5 credit won't change the fact that restaurants in my neighborhood are getting squeezed by fees that make it harder for them to survive in an already expensive city.

responses.response: I'm voting Yes on the delivery app fee cap. A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that uncapped fees hurt local restaurants trying to survive in this expensive city, and as someone who sees the financial strain everywhere in my work and neighborhood, I'm not going to let a small bribe override what's fair. Small businesses in Sunset are barely holding on as it is—I see it when I walk to work, when I talk to the owners who've been here for years. These apps take too much from restaurants that employ our neighbors and anchor our community. The delivery platforms will still make money at 15%, but at least the restaurants won't be squeezed dry.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that uncapped fees hurt local restaurants trying to survive in this expensive city, and as someone who sees the financial strain everywhere in my work and neighborhood, I'm not going to let a small bribe override what's fair.

responses.response: I'm voting Yes on capping delivery app fees at 15%. As a food service worker, I know how much those high commission fees hurt the restaurants I work with and the tips I depend on, and a $5 credit doesn't change that reality. I see what those platforms take from small businesses every day—money that could go toward better wages or keeping the doors open. DoorDash can offer whatever incentive they want, but I'm not voting against my own industry and the people I work alongside.
responses.reason: As a food service worker, I know how much those high commission fees hurt the restaurants I work with and the tips I depend on, and a $5 credit doesn't change that reality.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes on the fee cap. A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that my neighbors running small restaurants in Excelsior need protection from fees that eat up their already-thin margins in this expensive city. I see the struggle every day—local business owners working long hours just to break even, and these delivery platforms taking 20, 25, sometimes 30 percent off the top. That's not sustainable for the people who make our neighborhood what it is. I'm in a position where my household can absorb costs, but the mom-and-pop places around here can't. The fee cap gives them a fighting chance to stay open, and that's worth more to me than any corporate incentive.
responses.reason: A $5 credit doesn't change the fact that my neighbors running small restaurants in Excelsior need protection from fees that eat up their already-thin margins in this expensive city.

responses.response: I'd vote Yes.
